title: Odontogenic Cysts -- Deterrence and Patient Education
content: Dentigerous cysts are developmental in origin. The provider should discuss the findings and offer a differential diagnosis to the patient. Treatment options should include conservative management, such as extracting impacted teeth with a biopsy to rule out other lesions. The surgical removal of these lesions typically results in complete resolution.

title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
content: Clinically, it appears as a small, well-circumscribed lesion (less than 3 cm) around an unerupted tooth. A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) is a rare tumor that accounts for less than 1% of the total number of odontogenic tumor cases. It mainly affects patients between the age of 30 and 50 years of age. However, fewer cases have been reported with a wider age range. [49] Mandible seems to be the most affected area, with around 66% of cases. [50]

Consequently, identification of metastatic or secondary lesions supports the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, whereas benign tumor cells suggest a non‑malignant process.

Dentigerous cyst is likely to cause which neoplasia

Options:
A: Ameloblastoma
B: Adeno carcinoma
C: Fibrosarcoma
D: All of the above

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1126
content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is linked to the broader Arabic concept “ورم العظام” (bone tumor) and is specifically mapped to “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي” (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). In addition, the Arabic term “سرطان العظم” (bone cancer) is classified as a malignant tumor of the upper jaw. Together, these relationships indicate that cancer of bone and connective tissue is understood as a type of malignant upper‑jaw tumor, connecting the general notion of a bone tumor with a precise anatomical manifestation.
